item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've noticed that you like taking the 935s, kaktus. Good skip by the way

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks I hate it (because it does an excellent job frightening me) I don't know how you do it, but every one of your skips is a treat to read. I found myself really feeling for the Akot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a terrific addition to the End of Death Canon. Bravo. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly was rooting for the destruction of humanity. Huh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this one, especially after reading 4812. I find the idea of how humanity could evolve over long periods of time and all the while be shaped by tragedy really fascinating. The only question I had at the end was how the present-day foundation was able to outsmart the Akot and even prevent the reopening of their portal, when they are supposedly so advanced in relation to humans. If human technology is no match for Akot tech, and their minds are so superior to ours, how could humans have outplayed them so easily?
Once again this was a fantastic story, I just like to analyze. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, this just fully expands on the consequences of immortality and I love it

item.title: Typo
item.content:encoded:
We'll try and stay on this side of the Ark to avoid and of the falling debris.

You wrote “and” instead of “any”.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great story and world that i got very invested into! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… As if the horror provided by the EoD itself wasn't enough.

Very well-written, horrifying and just… Wrong story.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy god. I have never read an article this verbose and detailed. It just shudders me to the core just imagining it. Take my upvote…you genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how you do it kaktus, but every skip you post beats your previous one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slamming this skip into my bookmarks bar! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This whole thing is filled with great visuals, and the world building for EoD's future is very well done. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IIRC the medieval “dark ages” were actually fairly well recorded and has numerous scientific advances. Look up “Late Antiquity” at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did EoD ever establish a reason to shut down the Mole Rats?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously phenomenal work, man. I have to wonder if the number choice is a deliberate shout-out to 2935.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather long, but I think it carries the persistent horror of endless life rather well. I like these long-scale studies of a world far after death. +1

+ Minor points
- Minor points

they have better records of the medieval dark ages than they do for this period

The "dark ages" does not have an accepted scholarly definition within history, and is largely a cultural conception. (Other resources)

ΩK-Class ("End-of-Death") Scenario

Consider linking this to the canon hub.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of SCP-3003, in that it introduces a world, takes it apart, and creates a reason for it to be scary. Although I like it more from a world building perspective than a horror persective, this is still amazing. The desperation of the Akot is palpable and frightening.

+1

item.title: Re: The Hereafter
item.content:encoded: I'm an MCR nut. It's my job to keep things the way they should.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm so happy to finally see this on the site. I mean, ok Captain Kirby embracing an end of death article is literally the least surprising thing ever, but still.

I'm happy with what you did with the exploration log especially. I think it gives a nice addition of flavor to the Akot people in general, which builds the world nicely.

But overall what I really like about this piece takes end of death and makes it feel more grand. Generally, I tend to write this canon from a more personal perspective, focussing on a single character and letting readers extrapolate that character's experiences. I see this approach taken a lot in other EoD stuff as well, which I personally enjoy because I love character based narratives, and I don't want to see that trend stop.

However, this doesn't really take that route. It uses End of Death to tell a story that leans into its sci-fi elements more. The lore and the world wreak of more classical plot-driven science fiction stories, and I love to see that new approach applied to the canon in a way that still fits with the overall bleak and gray vibe that is exemplified in the rest of End of Death.

Also, as a side point - I like the irony of the Foundation essentially writing history reports on the future.

So yeah. Good stuff my man. Good stuff.

item.title: Re: The Hereafter
item.content:encoded: Damn how I do a guy up like that.

item.title: Re: The Hereafter
item.content:encoded: You misspelled his name. It's actually "Gerard" :V

item.title: The Hereafter
item.content:encoded:
Oh how wrong we were to think immortality meant never dying.
Gerard Way

This is my contribution to End of Death. I dunno if it's any good, but it was really enjoyable to write and be able to tie in a bunch of other stuff here and there. Thanks to Captain Kirby who told me to go back and look at this again, and convinced me to do a few things with it that ended up in the final product.

I hope you enjoy it. Thank you!
